tech_0432_fuel-fabrication-triso-fuel-fo.id_zh: 核能 > 核分裂 > 核燃料循環 > 核燃料循環前端 > 燃料製造：高溫反應爐用 TRISO 燃料

tech_0433_haleu-fuel-cycle.technology_status_summary_zh: 天然鈾所含可裂變鈾-235 不到 1%，其餘主要是不可裂變的鈾-238。核反應爐使用前，必須提高鈾-235 濃度。目前運轉中的反應爐機組使用低於 5% 的濃縮鈾；次世代設計則需要 5% 至 20%，其中包括約 50% 正在開發的 SMR 設計。此轉變需要為新的燃料類別建立供應鏈：低濃縮鈾增強型 LEU+（5%–10%），用於提升接近傳統設計之電廠的效率；以及高含量低濃縮鈾 HALEU（10%–20%），供更先進的反應爐使用。這需要專用的轉化與濃縮設施，可使用既有或新興技術，也需要新的運輸及廢棄物管理基礎設施。例如，HALEU 反應性較高，因此需要較小且符合臨界安全要求的運輸鋼瓶，以及專用的反轉化產線。考量更高的保安要求，法規與許可制度也須調整。目前 HALEU 商業供應稀少，產量主要由俄羅斯與中國掌握。多個 IEA 會員國正投入數十億資金建設國內基礎設施，以終止此項依賴。
tech_0433_haleu-fuel-cycle.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 安全且多元的 HALEU 燃料供應鏈，是部署次世代反應爐的關鍵，也有助於脫碳及提升能源安全。
tech_0433_haleu-fuel-cycle.technology_status_summary_en: Natural uranium contains less than 1% of fissile U-235, with the rest being non-fissile U-238. To fuel nuclear reactors, uranium must be enriched to increase the U-235 concentration. While current fleets operate below the 5% threshold, next-generation designs (including 50% of SMR designs in development) demand concentrations from 5% to 20%. This shift necessitates the development of supply chains for new fuel categories: LEU+ (Low-Enriched Uranium +, 5%–10%) for enhancing close-to-conventional plant efficiency and HALEU (High-Assay Low-Enriched Uranium. 10%–20%) for more advanced reactors. It requires specialized conversion and enrichment facilities (using existing or emerging technologies), new transport and waste management infrastructure (for example, HALEU’s higher reactivity demands smaller, criticality-safe transport cylinders and dedicated deconversion lines), but also modified regulations and licensing regimes, considering increased security requirements. HALEU remains commercially scarce, with production currently dominated by Russia and China. Several IEA member countries are investing billions into domestic infrastructure, in order to end this reliance.
tech_0433_haleu-fuel-cycle.market_dynamics_summary_en: Secure and diversified supply chains for HALEU fuel will be key to the deployment of next-generation reactors, a lever for decarbonisation and energy security.

tech_0433_haleu-fuel-cycle.NZErationale: Secure and diversified supply chains for HALEU fuel will be key to the deployment of next-generation reactors, a lever for decarbonisation and energy security.

initiatives.description: TRISO-X received a landmark NRC license to commercially fabricate TRISO fuel using HALEU in Tennessee

The UK’s Nuclear Transport Solutions and Westinghouse signed a strategic agreement to develop the "Pegasus" HALEU transport package. This partnership ensures safe domestic and international movement of advanced nuclear fuels.

The US Department of Energy awarded USD 2.7 billion to three companies to bolster domestic uranium enrichment. This 10-year investment focuses, among other things, on increasing LEU and HALEU production.
The UK government awarded USD 260 million to Urenco to build Europe’s first HALEU enrichment facility. This initiative aims to break Russia’s market monopoly by the early 2030s.
In June 2025, Centrus Energy successfully delivered 900 kilograms of HALEU to the US Department of Energy
Urenco USA enriched uranium to 8.5% (LEU+) in a pilot line designed for commercial operations.
Framatome and TerraPower partnered to develop a HALEU metallization pilot line in Washington (conversion of UO2 to a suitable form for the next steps of the fuel cycle).
Russia is currently the only commercial supplier of HALEU fuel in the world.

tech_0434_fuel-reprocessing-purex-proces.technology_name: Fuel reprocessing (PUREX process and new advanced techniques)
tech_0434_fuel-reprocessing-purex-proces.technology_name_zh: 燃料再處理（PUREX 製程與新型先進技術）
tech_0434_fuel-reprocessing-purex-proces.technology_status_summary_zh: 用過核燃料再處理旨在回收鈽與鈾供進一步能源生產，並減少高階放射性廢棄物體積。PUREX（鈽鈾萃取）是用過核燃料再處理的工業標準，利用濕法冶金溶劑萃取，將鈾與鈽從裂變產物中分離；鈽是在反應爐內由鈾-238 吸收中子後產生。之後再將鈽與鈾彼此分離，並分別轉換為 PuO₂ 及 UO₃／UO₂ 氧化物，供製成混合氧化物（MOX）燃料，在輕水式反應爐或快中子反應爐中再利用，或進行再次濃縮。剩餘高階廢棄物約占原燃料的 3%，含裂變產物及次要錒系元素，會以硼矽酸鹽玻璃固化，供長期貯存。為配合第四代快中子反應爐燃料循環、更新部分老舊再處理設施，尤其法國設施，以及提高對鈽擴散的抵抗能力，各國已投入大量資金開發 PUREX 替代技術。多數方案可視為 PUREX 的改良，而非顛覆性製程；但電化學高溫乾式再處理技術可能帶來較大變革。
tech_0434_fuel-reprocessing-purex-proces.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 先進燃料再處理是建立核能循環經濟的關鍵，可從用過核燃料回收最高 96% 的未利用能量，大幅延長鈾資源可用年限，並提高區域供應鏈韌性。
tech_0434_fuel-reprocessing-purex-proces.technology_status_summary_en:
Reprocessing used nuclear fuel aim to recover plutonium and uranium for additional energy production and to reduce the volume of high-level waste. The PUREX (Plutonium Uranium Extraction) process is the industrial standard for reprocessing spent nuclear fuel. It uses hydrometallurgical solvent extraction to separate uranium and plutonium, from fission products (plutonium is naturally produced in nuclear reactors when uranium-238 absorbs neutrons). Plutonium is later separated from uranium, and both are converted to oxides (PuO2 and UO₃/UO2) for reuse in MOX fuel (to be used in LWRs or fast reactors) or re-enrichment. The remaining high-level waste (3% of original fuel), containing fission products and minor actinides, is vitrified in borosilicate glass for long-term storage.

The need to adapt reprocessing techniques to the fuel cycles of Generation IV fast reactors, to soon renew certain old reprocessing units (particularly in France) and to enable greater proliferation resistance (plutonium) has led to significant investments and the development of alternatives to the PUREX process, most of which can be seen as advances in PUREX rather than disruptive processes, but Electrometallurgical Pyroprocessing tehchniques could bring some disruptions.
tech_0434_fuel-reprocessing-purex-proces.market_dynamics_summary_en: Advanced fuel reprocessing is vital for a circular nuclear economy: it recovers up to 96% of unused energy from spent fuel, drastically extending uranium resources and regional supply chains resilience.
tech_0434_fuel-reprocessing-purex-proces.description:

tech_0435_radioactive-waste-disposal.technology_status_summary_zh: 放射性廢棄物處置，是將鈾衍生廢棄物永久且最終與生物圈隔離，通常採深層地質處置。貯存屬於暫時、可取回且已實際部署的方案，需要持續主動監測；處置則可在不依賴人為介入下確保長期安全，但所需技術尚未完全發展成熟。廢棄物依放射性活度分類：低放射性廢棄物僅需最低程度的屏蔽，可埋設於近地表處置場；中放射性廢棄物須採顯著屏蔽，並處置於地下數十至數百公尺的專用工程設施；高放射性廢棄物須先在水池中主動冷卻數年，再進行玻璃固化，最後置於地下 500 m 以上的深層地質處置場，與環境隔離超過十萬年。低放射性廢棄物處置已進行數十年的常態化工業運轉；多國正積極部署中放射性廢棄物處置程序；高放射性廢棄物的最終處置尚未商業化，但北歐已開始興建首批最終處置場。
tech_0435_radioactive-waste-disposal.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 若核能要成為全球脫碳經濟的支柱，所有類型放射性廢棄物都必須具備成熟的處置程序；尤其全球正力求在 2050 年前將核能裝置容量提高至三倍。
tech_0435_radioactive-waste-disposal.technology_status_summary_en: Radioactive waste disposal is the permanent, final isolation of uranium-derived waste from the biosphere, typically in deep geological repositories. Unlike storage, which is a temporary, retrievable and already deployed solution requiring active monitoring, disposal ensures long-term safety without human intervention, but the necessary techniques are not yet fully developed. Waste is categorized by activity: Low-Level (requires minimal shielding, buried in near-surface repositories), Intermediate-Level (requires significant shielding, and disposal in specialized engineered facilities at depths of dozens to hundreds of meters.), and High-Level (necessitates active cooling in water pools for years, followed by vitrification, and final disposal in Deep Geological Repositories, 500+ meters underground, to isolate it for 100,000+ years). Low-level wate disposal has been in routine industrial operation for decades, Intermediate-waste disposal processes are being actively deployed in several countries, but processes for High-Level wate disposal are not yet commercialy available, but first final repositories are being built in Scandinavia.
tech_0435_radioactive-waste-disposal.market_dynamics_summary_en: Mature processes for the disposal of all types of radioactive waste are necessary for nuclear power to become a  backbone of a decarbonized global economy, particularly as the world strives to triple its nuclear capacity by 2050.
tech_0435_radioactive-waste-disposal.description: Radioactive waste disposal is the permanent, final isolation of uranium-derived waste from the biosphere, typically in deep geological repositories. Unlike storage, which is a temporary, retrievable and already deployed solution requiring active monitoring, disposal ensures long-term safety without human intervention, but the necessary techniques are not yet fully developed. Waste is categorized by activity: Low-Level (requires minimal shielding, buried in near-surface repositories), Intermediate-Level (requires significant shielding, and disposal in specialized engineered facilities at depths of dozens to hundreds of meters.), and High-Level (necessitates active cooling in water pools for years, followed by vitrification, and final disposal in Deep Geological Repositories, 500+ meters underground, to isolate it for 100,000+ years). Low-level wate disposal has been in routine industrial operation for decades, Intermediate-waste disposal processes are being actively deployed in several countries, but processes for High-Level wate disposal are not yet commercialy available, but first final repositories are being built in Scandinavia.
tech_0435_radioactive-waste-disposal.NZErationale: Mature processes for the disposal of all types of radioactive waste are necessary for nuclear power to become a  backbone of a decarbonized global economy, particularly as the world strives to triple its nuclear capacity by 2050.

initiatives.description: Construction of Sweden’s first final repository for spent nuclear fuel (high-level) began in Forsmark in January 2025. Disposal operations are scheduled to commence in the late 2030s.

Cigéo is the French deep geological disposal project for high-level and long-lived intermediate-level waste. Located 500 meters deep in the Meuse/Haute-Marne region, it is slated for commissioning around 2035 with a 100-year operational lifespan.
Expansion of the existing repository in Forsmark, dedicated to low and intermediate-level waste, officially started with blasting in January 2025.
Finland's Onkalo repository is the first world-first deep geological facility for high-level waste in construction. In August 2024, it began trial runs.

tech_0435_radioactive-waste-disposal.id: Nuclear > Fission > Nuclear fuel cycles > Back-end of the fuel cycle > Radioactive waste disposal
tech_0435_radioactive-waste-disposal.id_zh: 核能 > 核分裂 > 核燃料循環 > 核燃料循環後端 > 放射性廢棄物處置

tech_0436_thorium-fuel-cycle.technology_status_summary_zh: 釷-232 可在部分反應爐中作為可孕材料。釷在地殼中的含量約為鈾的三至四倍，廣泛存在於岩石與土壤中，是更豐富的潛在核能資源。釷本身與鈾-238 一樣不可裂變，但受中子照射後可轉換為可裂變的鈾-233，並能維持連鎖反應。因此，釷燃料循環需要先以鈾-233、鈾-235 或鈽-239 等可裂變驅動燃料提供多餘中子，照射釷-232 進行增殖。此過程先產生鏷-233，約一個月後自然衰變為鈾-233。為維持良好的中子經濟，使反應爐產生的燃料多於消耗量，須持續以化學方式將鏷-233 與其他產物分離。在傳統反應爐中，釷可製成固態氧化物燃料丸（Th-MOX）；釷尤其適用於熔鹽反應爐（MSR），可溶於液態氟鹽中。這類液態燃料反應爐效率特別高，因為可就地利用或以化學方式分離鏷-233。過去五十多年來，多種反應爐類型均研究過釷燃料潛力；隨著熔鹽反應爐及加速器驅動次臨界反應爐進展，目前全球再度重視此路徑。中國目前在釷燃料循環開發方面明顯領先；擁有最大釷資源的印度，也計畫在長期核能方案中採用此燃料循環。
tech_0436_thorium-fuel-cycle.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 釷燃料循環可顯著降低對鈾供應的依賴，且所使用的釷資源更豐富、分布於更多國家。
tech_0436_thorium-fuel-cycle.technology_status_summary_en: Thorium-232 can be used as the fertile material in some reactors. Thorium is approximately three to four times more abundant in the Earth's crust than uranium and is found in most rocks and soils, making it a much more plentiful potential resource for nuclear power generation. Thorium itself is, as Uranium-238, not fissile, but when irradiated with neutrons, it converts into Uranium-233, which is fissile and can easily sustain a chain reaction. Thorium fuel cycles thus require initial neutron irradiation to convert Th-232, obtained through a fissile driver—U-233, U-235, or Pu-239—which provide surplus neutrons for breeding. Protactinium-233 is firstly produced, and naturally turns into Uranium-233 after about a month. Protactinium-233 continuously need to undergo chemical separation from the other products, to maintain the high neutron economy necessary to produce more fuel than the reactor consumes. In conventional reactors, it can be processed into solid oxide fuel pellets (Th-MOX), but it is uniquely suited for Molten Salt Reactors (MSRs) where it is dissolved in liquid fluoride salts. These liquid reactors are particularly efficient because they allow for the "in-situ" use or chemical separation of Protactinium-233. Over five decades of research have explored the potential of thorium as a nuclear fuel across multiple reactor types. Today, this effort is seeing a global resurgence driven by advancements in molten salt and accelerator-driven subcritical reactors. To date, China has taken a significant lead in the development of this type of fuel cycle, and, India, which has the largest thorium resources, plan to rely on this fuel cycle in its long-term nuclear programme.
tech_0436_thorium-fuel-cycle.market_dynamics_summary_en: A thorium fuel cycle significantly reduces dependence on uranium supplies and relies on a material that is more abundant and available in a greater number of countries.

initiatives.description: China's TMSR-LF1 experimental molten salt reactor successfully converted thorium-232 into fissile uranium-233. It's currently the world’s only operational molten salt reactor to have successfully integrated thorium fuel into its energy conversion cycle.

India commenced fuel loading for its 500 MWe Prototype Fast Breeder Reactor at Kalpakkam, which could pontentially use Thorium as fertile cover around MOX fuel. The facility is a key component of India’s three-stage nuclear strategy, based on Thorium fuel cycles.

tech_0437_generation-iii-iii-large-react.technology_name_zh: 第三代／第三代加強型大型反應爐
tech_0437_generation-iii-iii-large-react.technology_status_summary_zh: 第三代與第三代加強型核分裂反應爐，是目前已部署核能技術的最新演進，吸收前幾代反應爐、重大事故，以及內外部威脅的經驗。這些先進反應爐著重提升核安與保安，採用強化圍阻體及被動安全系統，利用重力、自然對流等自然力，減少對主動控制及外部電力的依賴。較早期的第三代反應爐自 1996 年起已在日本運轉，此世代預計至少運轉至 2070 年代。第三代反應爐累積的經驗也開始為 SMR 開發鋪路，包括以標準化設計簡化許可與施工，以及採模組化組裝縮短工期。這些反應爐也透過提高燃耗及將運轉壽命延長至最高 60 年，改善燃料利用效率。許多第三代反應爐亦具備負載追隨能力，可彈性調整輸出，協助維持電網穩定。此世代幾乎全部採水冷卻，多為壓水式反應爐（PWR）與沸水式反應爐（BWR），使用熱中子能譜；核分裂釋出的中子透過水或石墨等中子緩速劑減速，以利鈾-235 分裂。燃料主要為濃縮度 3% 至 5% 的氧化鈾，製成燃料丸並排列於燃料棒中。
tech_0437_generation-iii-iii-large-react.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 第三代反應爐無疑將在核能推動能源結構脫碳方面扮演最重要角色。第二代反應爐即將達到使用年限，而第四代反應爐的開發時程過長，難以在 2050 年淨零目標中發揮顯著作用；為達成該目標，核能裝置容量須增加一倍。
tech_0437_generation-iii-iii-large-react.technology_status_summary_en:
Generation III and III+ nuclear fission reactors represent the latest deployed evolution in nuclear power technology, building on lessons from previous generations and major incidents, from internal and external threats. These advanced reactors emphasize enhanced safety and security, incorporating reinforced containment structures and passive safety systems that rely on natural forces such as gravity and convection, reducing dependence on active controls and external power. The older Generation III reactor have been operating in Japan since 1996, and this generation will be operational until at least the 2070s. 

The experience gained with this generation has begun to pave the way for the development of SMRs: Designs are standardized to streamline licensing and construction, modular assembly for faster build times. These reactors also have higher fuel efficiency through increased burn-up rates and extended operating lifespans of up to 60 years. Many Generation III reactors are also designed for load-following, enabling flexible power output adjustments to support grid stability. 

Almost all reactors from this generation are water cooled (mostly PWRs and BWRs), use thermal neutron spectrum (neutrons emitted by fission reactions are slowed down by a moderator (water or graphite) to facilitate the fission of Uranium 235) and use a used primarily enriched uranium (3–5% U-235) oxide in the forms of pellets arranged in fuel rods as fuel.
tech_0437_generation-iii-iii-large-react.market_dynamics_summary_en: Generation III reactors will undoubtedly play the biggest role in decarbonising the energy mix through nuclear power: Generation II reactors will soon reach the end of their lifespan, and the development timelines for Generation IV reactors are too long to envisage them playing a significant role in achieving Net Zero by 2050, for which installed nuclear capacity will need to be doubled.
tech_0437_generation-iii-iii-large-react.description:
Generation III and III+ nuclear fission reactors represent the latest deployed evolution in nuclear power technology, building on lessons from previous generations and major incidents, from internal and external threats. These advanced reactors emphasize enhanced safety and security, incorporating reinforced containment structures and passive safety systems that rely on natural forces such as gravity and convection, reducing dependence on active controls and external power. The older Generation III reactor have been operating in Japan since 1996, and this generation will be operational until at least the 2070s. 

The experience gained with this generation has begun to pave the way for the development of SMRs: Designs are standardized to streamline licensing and construction, modular assembly for faster build times. These reactors also have higher fuel efficiency through increased burn-up rates and extended operating lifespans of up to 60 years. Many Generation III reactors are also designed for load-following, enabling flexible power output adjustments to support grid stability. 

Almost all reactors from this generation are water cooled (mostly PWRs and BWRs), use thermal neutron spectrum (neutrons emitted by fission reactions are slowed down by a moderator (water or graphite) to facilitate the fission of Uranium 235) and use a used primarily enriched uranium (3–5% U-235) oxide in the forms of pellets arranged in fuel rods as fuel.
tech_0437_generation-iii-iii-large-react.NZErationale: Generation III reactors will undoubtedly play the biggest role in decarbonising the energy mix through nuclear power: Generation II reactors will soon reach the end of their lifespan, and the development timelines for Generation IV reactors are too long to envisage them playing a significant role in achieving Net Zero by 2050, for which installed nuclear capacity will need to be doubled.

initiatives.description: The first Generation III reactor starded operation in 1996 in Japan. Since then, dozens of reactors have been built and have begun operating in several countries, including France, the United States, Russia, China and Korea.
The IAEA lists the 413 reactors in operation worldwide. The vast majority of them are Generation II, but a growing proportion are Generation III or III+.

tech_0438_generation-iv-large-reactors-f.technology_name: Generation IV large reactors : fast neutron spectrum
tech_0438_generation-iv-large-reactors-f.technology_name_zh: 第四代大型反應爐：快中子能譜
tech_0438_generation-iv-large-reactors-f.technology_status_summary_zh: 若第三代／第三代加強型反應爐是第二代技術的延續，第四代反應爐則代表相對現行設計的根本性技術轉變，預期約在 2050 年實現工業部署。這些系統旨在滿足永續性、安全性及經濟競爭力的嚴格標準，操作溫度為 500 至 1 000 °C，顯著高於現行反應爐，可支援熱化學製氫等應用，且多數採封閉式燃料循環。第四代核能系統國際論壇（GIF）於 2001 年成立，目前由 13 個會員國及歐洲原子能共同體組成，協調相關研發；十五年間投入超過 60 億美元，主要由日本、法國及美國出資。GIF 選出六項先進技術進一步開發，其中三項為快中子反應爐：以液態金屬鈉或鉛冷卻的鈉冷式快中子反應爐（SFR）與鉛冷式快中子反應爐（LFR），以及氣冷式快中子反應爐（GFR）。不同於傳統熱中子能譜反應爐，快中子反應爐不使用緩速劑降低核分裂中子的速度，因此可從未濃縮鈾增殖新燃料，但也使製程更複雜、成本更高，並帶來新的安全挑戰。這些設計背後的研發並非全新，但第四代所發展的設計形成重要轉折。
tech_0438_generation-iv-large-reactors-f.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 大型第四代反應爐的開發與部署時程可能過長，難以在 2050 年前對電力結構產生顯著影響；但其小型模組化反應爐（SMR）應用可能較早商業化。
tech_0438_generation-iv-large-reactors-f.technology_status_summary_en:
If Generation III/III+ reactors were a continuation of those of Generation II, Generation IV reactors represent a radical technological shift from current designs and are expected to achieve industrial deployment around 2050. These systems aim to meet stringent criteria for sustainability, safety and economic competitiveness. These designs operate at significantly higher temperatures (500°C to 1000°C) than today’s reactors, enabling applications such as thermochemical hydrogen production and most of them employ closed fuel cycles. 

An international collaboration, the Generation IV International Forum (GIF), launched in 2001 and now comprising 13 member countries plus Euratom, coordinates R&D efforts – supported by investments exceeding USD 6 billion over 15 years, primarily funded by Japan, France, and the United States – and selected six advanced technologies for further development, three of them are fast neutron reactors, using liquid metals (sodium or lead, SFRs and LFRs) or gas (GFRs) as coolant.  

Unlike conventional thermal neutron spectrum reactors, these fast reactors do not use a moderator to slow down the neutrons emitted by the fission reaction, enabling the breeding of new fuel from unenriched uranium, but complicating the process, increasing costs and posing new challenges in terms of safety. The R&D behind these designs is not new, but the designs developed in this fourth generation mark a turning point.
tech_0438_generation-iv-large-reactors-f.market_dynamics_summary_en: The timeline for the development and deployment of large Generation IV reactors seems too long to have a significant impact on the electricity mix by 2050, but their small-scale modular applications (SMRs) could be commercialised sooner.
tech_0438_generation-iv-large-reactors-f.description:
If Generation III/III+ reactors were a continuation of those of Generation II, Generation IV reactors represent a radical technological shift from current designs and are expected to achieve industrial deployment around 2050. These systems aim to meet stringent criteria for sustainability, safety and economic competitiveness. These designs operate at significantly higher temperatures (500°C to 1000°C) than today’s reactors, enabling applications such as thermochemical hydrogen production and most of them employ closed fuel cycles. 

An international collaboration, the Generation IV International Forum (GIF), launched in 2001 and now comprising 13 member countries plus Euratom, coordinates R&D efforts – supported by investments exceeding USD 6 billion over 15 years, primarily funded by Japan, France, and the United States – and selected six advanced technologies for further development, three of them are fast neutron reactors, using liquid metals (sodium or lead, SFRs and LFRs) or gas (GFRs) as coolant.  

Unlike conventional thermal neutron spectrum reactors, these fast reactors do not use a moderator to slow down the neutrons emitted by the fission reaction, enabling the breeding of new fuel from unenriched uranium, but complicating the process, increasing costs and posing new challenges in terms of safety. The R&D behind these designs is not new, but the designs developed in this fourth generation mark a turning point.
tech_0438_generation-iv-large-reactors-f.NZErationale: The timeline for the development and deployment of large Generation IV reactors seems too long to have a significant impact on the electricity mix by 2050, but their small-scale modular applications (SMRs) could be commercialised sooner.

initiatives.description: In February 2021, the China Experimental Fast Reactor (CEFR) restarted and began high-power grid-connected operation after a refuelling and maintenance outage. The CEFR, a 65 MW thermal sodium-cooled fast reactor, was built with Russian assistance and achieved first criticality in 2010. Based on its experience, China started construction of the CFR-600, a 600 MWe demonstration fast reactor, in December 2017 in Fujian province. The CFR1000, a commercial-scale reactor (1000–1200 MWe), could begin construction in 2028, with operation expected around 2034.

Rosatom started development of SFRs in the 1960s, which resulted in the BN-600 and BN-800 pilot reactors, located in Beloyarsk, operating since 1980 and 2015, and operationauthorized until 2040. Even if these reactors do not fully meet the GenIV criteria, the related operational experience will feed the development of Russian GenIV reactors.

The construction of the Rosatom’s BREST-OD-300 LFR demonstration reactor continue. The reactor is expected to begin operation in the coming years, with a focus on performance testing before transitioning to commercial use. If successful, a larger 1,200 MWe version (BR-1200) may follow.

Several fast reactors (mainly sodium cooled) were in operation well before the 2000s, but none of them met the Gen IV criteria. However, they form a fundamental basis that greatly feeds into R&D in Gen IV reactors.

Following the operational experience from the BN-600 and BN-800 pilot reactors, Russia has obtained the environmental permit for the construction of the BN-1200M commercial sodium-cooled fast breeder reactor at the Beloyarsk site. Construction is expected to start in 2027, following regulatory approvals.

tech_0439_generation-iv-large-reactors-t.technology_name: Generation IV large reactors : thermal neutron spectrum
tech_0439_generation-iv-large-reactors-t.technology_name_zh: 第四代大型反應爐：熱中子能譜
tech_0439_generation-iv-large-reactors-t.technology_status_summary_zh: 若第三代／第三代加強型反應爐是第二代技術的延續，第四代反應爐則代表相對現行設計的根本性技術轉變，預期約在 2050 年實現工業部署。這些系統旨在滿足永續性、安全性及經濟競爭力的嚴格標準，操作溫度為 500 至 1 000 °C，顯著高於現行反應爐，可支援熱化學製氫等應用，且多數採封閉式燃料循環。第四代核能系統國際論壇（GIF）於 2001 年成立，目前由 13 個會員國及歐洲原子能共同體組成，協調相關研發；十五年間的投資額超過十億美元，但英文原文未列具體數字，主要由日本、法國及美國出資。GIF 選出六項先進技術進一步開發，其中三項主要採用傳統熱中子能譜；核分裂中子先由水或石墨等緩速劑減速，以利鈾-235 分裂。這三項技術為：超臨界水冷式反應爐（SCWR），以超過水臨界點的壓力與溫度運轉；部分熔鹽反應爐（MSR），設計形式多樣，例如氟鹽冷卻高溫反應爐（FHR）結合 MSR 與高溫氣冷式反應爐（HTGR），以氟鹽為冷卻劑並使用 TRISO 燃料，以及使用釷燃料的 TMSR；還有超高溫反應爐（VHTR），使用氦氣冷卻與 TRISO 燃料，可能應用於多種產業，但並非針對大型發電用途設計。
tech_0439_generation-iv-large-reactors-t.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 大型第四代反應爐的開發與部署時程可能過長，難以在 2050 年前對電力結構產生顯著影響；但其小型模組化反應爐（SMR）應用可能較早商業化。
tech_0439_generation-iv-large-reactors-t.technology_status_summary_en:
If Generation III/III+ reactors were a continuation of those of Generation II, Generation IV reactors represent a radical technological shift from current designs and are expected to achieve industrial deployment around 2050. These systems aim to meet stringent criteria for sustainability, safety and economic competitiveness. These designs operate at significantly higher temperatures (500°C to 1000°C) than today’s reactors, enabling applications such as thermochemical hydrogen production and most of them employ closed fuel cycles. 

An international collaboration, the Generation IV International Forum (GIF), launched in 2001 and now comprising 13 member countries plus Euratom, coordinates R&D efforts – supported by investments exceeding USD billion over 15 years, primarily funded by Japan, France, and the United States – and selected six advanced technologies for further development, three of them are primilary using conventional thermal neutron spectrum (neutrons emitted by fission reactions are slowed down by a moderator (water or graphite) to facilitate the fission of Uranium 235): the Super Critical Water Reactors (SCWRs, cooled by water, operating at pression and temperature above the related crirical point), some Molten Salt Reactors (MSRs, with varying designs: for example: FHRs use a mix between MSRs and HTGRs use fluoride salts as a coolant and TRISO fuel, and TMSR, using Thorium fuel), and the Very High Temperature Reactors (VHTRs, helium coolant, TRISO fuel with potential application in several industries, but not designed for large scale applications).
tech_0439_generation-iv-large-reactors-t.market_dynamics_summary_en: The timeline for the development and deployment of large Generation IV reactors seems too long to have a significant impact on the electricity mix by 2050, but their small-scale modular applications (SMRs) could be commercialised sooner.

If Generation III/III+ reactors were a continuation of those of Generation II, Generation IV reactors represent a radical technological shift from current designs and are expected to achieve industrial deployment around 2050. These systems aim to meet stringent criteria for sustainability, safety and economic competitiveness. These designs operate at significantly higher temperatures (500°C to 1000°C) than today’s reactors, enabling applications such as thermochemical hydrogen production and most of them employ closed fuel cycles. 

An international collaboration, the Generation IV International Forum (GIF), launched in 2001 and now comprising 13 member countries plus Euratom, coordinates R&D efforts – supported by investments exceeding USD billion over 15 years, primarily funded by Japan, France, and the United States – and selected six advanced technologies for further development, three of them are primilary using conventional thermal neutron spectrum (neutrons emitted by fission reactions are slowed down by a moderator (water or graphite) to facilitate the fission of Uranium 235): the Super Critical Water Reactors (SCWRs, cooled by water, operating at pression and temperature above the related crirical point), some Molten Salt Reactors (MSRs, with varying designs: for example: FHRs use a mix between MSRs and HTGRs use fluoride salts as a coolant and TRISO fuel, and TMSR, using Thorium fuel), and the Very High Temperature Reactors (VHTRs, helium coolant, TRISO fuel with potential application in several industries, but not designed for large scale applications).
tech_0439_generation-iv-large-reactors-t.NZErationale: The timeline for the development and deployment of large Generation IV reactors seems too long to have a significant impact on the electricity mix by 2050, but their small-scale modular applications (SMRs) could be commercialised sooner.

initiatives.description: There are no Supercritical water cooled reactors beyond the design phase, but Canada, China, Japan, Russia are developing reactors designed for large scale applications.
In the second half of the 20th century, the US led R&D on MSRs, but without achieving large-scale applications.
The Oak Ridge National Laboratory in the US dedicates part of its R&D to molten salt reactors, particularly fluoride-salt-cooled high-temperature reactors.
Several start-ups are developing molten salt SMRs, which could provide experimental knowledge for the development of large reactors.

In early 2026, the China National Nuclear Corporation announced the start of construction of a 2GWe nuclear power plant using a Generation IV high temperature gas cooled unit alongside two conventional units. This will become the first large-scale Generation IV reactor (>500MWe) in construction in the world. China is also operating some smaller reactors which could feed into the design of other Generation IV large reactors (including the CEFR experimental reactor, and the HTR-PM SMR).

In 2023, China began operating a high-temperature gas-cooled SMR (HTR-PM). While the reactor design is intended for small-scale and modular applications, the associated experience could be decisive for scaling up to larger designs.

tech_0440_water-cooled-smr.technology_name_zh: 水冷式小型模組化反應爐（SMR）
tech_0440_water-cooled-smr.technology_status_summary_zh: 小型模組化反應爐（SMR）是通常低於 300 MWe 的緊湊型核反應爐，設計為在工廠製造並於場址組裝。相較傳統反應爐，SMR 可能縮短工期，部署方式也更具彈性，特別適合偏遠地區、電網彈性不足地區，以及供熱等特定工業用途。全球有超過 130 種 SMR 設計正在開發，但尚無任何設計完成大規模複製部署。壓水式與沸水式 SMR 將大型第二代及第三代反應爐數十年使用的原理縮小並模組化，以加壓水或沸水作為冷卻劑，並採熱中子能譜。儘管大型水冷式反應爐已有豐富運轉經驗，目前仍無任何水冷式 SMR 設計在陸地完成商業規模示範。
tech_0440_water-cooled-smr.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 小型模組化反應爐（SMR）是推動脫碳的重要工具，可加快核能部署，並促進工業、製氫、CCUS 及區域供熱等關鍵部門電氣化。SMR 也可部署於部分孤立地區或資料中心附近，協助提高電網彈性。
tech_0440_water-cooled-smr.technology_status_summary_en:
A Small Modular Reactor (SMR) is a compact nuclear reactor (typically under 300 MWe) designed for factory production and on-site assembling. SMRs could offer faster construction than traditional reactors, and more flexible deployment. They are particularly suited to grid application, in remote or not flexible enough areas, and to specific industrial applications (heat production). Although over 130 SMR designs are under development worldwide, not a single one has yet been replicated or deployed on a large scale.

Pressurised and Boiling water SMRs would apply the principle used for several decades in large Generation II and III reactors  –  where high-pressure water or heat is used as a coolant, with a thermal neutron spectrum – to small-scale, modular applications. Despite extensive experience with this type of reactor in large-scale settings, no SMR design has yet led to a commercial demonstration on-land.
tech_0440_water-cooled-smr.market_dynamics_summary_en: SMRs are an important lever for decarbonization, enabling faster deployment of nuclear power and electrification of key sectors (industry, hydrogen production, CCUS, district heat). They can also help improve grid flexibility, for example by being deployed in certain isolated regions or near data centers.
tech_0440_water-cooled-smr.description:
A Small Modular Reactor (SMR) is a compact nuclear reactor (typically under 300 MWe) designed for factory production and on-site assembling. SMRs could offer faster construction than traditional reactors, and more flexible deployment. They are particularly suited to grid application, in remote or not flexible enough areas, and to specific industrial applications (heat production). Although over 130 SMR designs are under development worldwide, not a single one has yet been replicated or deployed on a large scale.

Pressurised and Boiling water SMRs would apply the principle used for several decades in large Generation II and III reactors  –  where high-pressure water or heat is used as a coolant, with a thermal neutron spectrum – to small-scale, modular applications. Despite extensive experience with this type of reactor in large-scale settings, no SMR design has yet led to a commercial demonstration on-land.
tech_0440_water-cooled-smr.NZErationale: SMRs are an important lever for decarbonization, enabling faster deployment of nuclear power and electrification of key sectors (industry, hydrogen production, CCUS, district heat). They can also help improve grid flexibility, for example by being deployed in certain isolated regions or near data centers.

initiatives.description: The Akademik Lomonosov floating small modular PWR (RITM-200M) started commercial operation in 2020. In 5 years (2025), it has generated over 1 billion kWh, and currently supplies about 60 % of the region’s electricity.

Rolls-Royce SMR announced an exclusive global turbine-systems partnership with Siemens Energy, where Siemens will supply steam turbines, generators and auxiliary systems for its Generation 3+ SMRs under development
NuScale supports TVA and ENTRA1 Energy’s landmark plan to deploy up to 6 GW of SMRs, by assuring the supply of its NRC-approved NuScale Power Modules for multiple plants.

Linglong One (ACP100) is the first land-based SMR to pass an IAEA safety review. A demonstration unit in Hainan is under construction, with civil works largely completed: cold functionnal tests have been completed in 2025. This demonstration is the first step in a long-term roadmap for the deployment of associated design in Hainan Province.

Rosatom, a state-owned Russian nuclear company, is constructing its first land-based RITM-200N SMR for commissioning in 2028. In Uzbekistan, preparatory work is underway for RITM-200N units at a new integrated site.

Last Energy, a US based start-up is developping a pressurised water microreactor (20MWe), and plans to deploy several dozen reactors to power data centers in the US and plan to obtain a site license for a project in the UK.
EDF has announced a simplification and optimisation of the design of its Nuward small modular reactor (SMR) and is relaunching its development.
Vattenfall (Sweden) chose GE Vernova (BWRs) and Rolls-Royce SMR (PWRs) to build Sweden’s first new nuclear reactors in 40 years by 2035.

Ontario province, Canada, and Ontario Power Generation approved the construction of a BWRX‑300 small modular reactor (SMR) unit (300 MW), which GE Hitachi predicts will be the first SMR in the Western world. This reactor has also been selected for potential deployment in several other countries (United States, Poland, Sweden and Estonia).
The US DOE is supporting the deployment of water-cooled SMRs through federal cost-sharing funding of demonstration projects

Holtec and Hyundai Engineering & Construction aim to deploy a 10 GW fleet of SMR-300 pressurized water SMRs in the U.S. by the 2030s, starting with the Palisades site in Michigan, targeting 2030 for first operation.

NuScale Power, a US-based company, has been actively pursuing a SMR project in Romania, which finally reached a final investment decision in 2026. NuScale has also signed MoU and co-operation agreements with Bulgaria, Canada, the Czech Republic, Ghana, Indonesia, Korea, Poland, Ukraine and the United States.

Rolls-Royce SMR was selected in the Great British Nuclear SMR competition, and Rolls Royce signed an early works agreement with Czech energy group ČEZ. It is also in the final stages of a utility selection process in Sweden.
The European Commission unveiled a unified strategy to bring Europe’s first SMRs online by the early 2030s. Most of the related selected reactors are water-cooled.

Calogena, a French start-up, secured nearly €100M from France 2030 and private investors to industrialize its heat-only SMR (mostly district heat). Targeting 2030 deployment, the funding accelerates detailed design and expansion across France and Finland.

tech_0440_water-cooled-smr.id: Nuclear > Fission > Small modular reactors > Water-cooled SMR

tech_0441_high-temperature-gas-cooled-sm.technology_name_zh: 高溫氣冷式小型模組化反應爐（SMR）
tech_0441_high-temperature-gas-cooled-sm.technology_status_summary_zh: 小型模組化反應爐（SMR）是通常低於 300 MWe 的緊湊型核反應爐，設計為在工廠製造並於場址組裝。相較傳統反應爐，SMR 可能縮短工期，部署方式也更具彈性，特別適合偏遠地區、電網彈性不足地區，以及供熱等特定工業用途。全球有超過 130 種 SMR 設計正在開發，部分已興建或運轉，但尚無任何設計完成大規模複製部署。高溫氣冷式 SMR 使用氦氣作為冷卻劑，操作溫度通常為 700 至 900 °C，部分設計可達 950 至 1 000 °C，可提升熱效率並支援特定工業用途。此類反應爐通常使用 TRISO 燃料，動力轉換系統可採傳統朗肯循環或布雷頓循環。目前尚無高溫氣冷式 SMR 設計完成大規模複製部署，但中國已有一項設計完成示範並投入商業運轉。
tech_0441_high-temperature-gas-cooled-sm.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 小型模組化反應爐（SMR）是推動脫碳的重要工具，可加快核能部署，並促進工業、製氫及 CCUS 等關鍵部門電氣化。SMR 也可部署於部分孤立地區或資料中心附近，協助提高電網彈性。
tech_0441_high-temperature-gas-cooled-sm.technology_status_summary_en:
A Small Modular Reactor (SMR) is a compact nuclear reactor (typically under 300 MWe) designed for factory production and on-site assembling. SMRs could offer faster construction than traditional reactors, and more flexible deployment. They are particularly suited to grid application, in remote or not flexible enough areas, and to specific industrial applications (heat production). Although over 130 SMR designs are under development worldwide, with some of under construction or already operational, not a single one has yet been replicated or deployed on a large scale.

High-temperature gas cooled small modural reactors use helium gas as the coolant and operates at very high temperatures (typically 700–900 °C, sometimes up to 950–1000 °C), enhancing thermal efficiency improvments, and specific industrial applications. These reactors generally use TRISO fuel and can be based on conventional Rankine cycles or Brayton cycles. At present, no high-temperature gas-cooled SMR design has been replicated and deployed on a large scale, but one have been demonstrated and is currently commercialy operated in China.
tech_0441_high-temperature-gas-cooled-sm.market_dynamics_summary_en: SMRs are an important lever for decarbonization, enabling faster deployment of nuclear power and electrification of key sectors (industry, hydrogen production, CCUS). They can also help improve grid flexibility, for example by being deployed in certain isolated regions or near data centers.

They are particularly suited to grid application, in remote or not flexible enough areas, and to specific industrial applications (heat production). Although over 130 SMR designs are under development worldwide, with some of under construction or already operational, not a single one has yet been replicated or deployed on a large scale.

High-temperature gas cooled small modural reactors use helium gas as the coolant and operates at very high temperatures (typically 700–900 °C, sometimes up to 950–1000 °C), enhancing thermal efficiency improvments, and specific industrial applications. These reactors generally use TRISO fuel and can be based on conventional Rankine cycles or Brayton cycles. At present, no high-temperature gas-cooled SMR design has been replicated and deployed on a large scale, but one have been demonstrated and is currently commercialy operated in China.
tech_0441_high-temperature-gas-cooled-sm.NZErationale: SMRs are an important lever for decarbonization, enabling faster deployment of nuclear power and electrification of key sectors (industry, hydrogen production, CCUS). They can also help improve grid flexibility, for example by being deployed in certain isolated regions or near data centers.

initiatives.description: The Polish Centre for Nuclear Research is developing a high-temperature gas cooled SMR
The JAEA is currently conducing a R&D program on HTGR, with some prossible small modular applications.
Since the 2010s, General Atomics is developping a gas-cooled fast SMR, the Energy Multiplier Module (EM2).
In 2024, the SMR HTR-PM was connected to a district heating network to heat 2,000 homes.
China plans to replicate the HTR-PM reactor, using the gained experience to develop a 600 MWe plant using six HTR-PM units (HTR-PM600).
UK’s NNL and Japan’s JAEA partnered to advance coated particle fuel for high-temperature gas-cooled reactors.

South Africa has ended the care-and-maintenance status of its Pebble Bed Modular Reactor (PBMR) project, transferring it from Eskom (national utiliy) to Necsa (national nuclear energy corporation) to revive R&D and commercialize a  high-temperature, TRISO-fueled reactor technology. The move aims to address 16 years of stalled SMR and fuel cycle progress.
Founded in 2019, Radiant nuclear develops a portable, helium-cooled nuclear microreactor (1MWe) designed to replace diesel generators in remote areas, military bases, hostipals and data centers.

China’s HTR-PM high-temperature pebble-bed reactor at Shidao Bay entered commercial operation in December 2023. The twin-module, 210 MWe plant uses helium coolant and is designed to co-generate heat (500°C steam) and electricity.
In 2025, X-energy, a US based start-up received a USD 700 million funding round, to support its ambitious deployment roadmap.
Jimmy Energy, a french start-up, plan to deliver its first 20 to 60 MWth reactors in 2027. It is intended for industrial heat production.

tech_0441_high-temperature-gas-cooled-sm.id: Nuclear > Fission > Small modular reactors > High-temperature gas-cooled SMR
tech_0441_high-temperature-gas-cooled-sm.id_zh: 核能 > 核分裂 > 小型模組化反應爐 > 高溫氣冷式小型模組化反應爐（SMR）

tech_0442_sodium-fast-smr.technology_name_zh: 鈉冷式快中子小型模組化反應爐（SMR）
tech_0442_sodium-fast-smr.technology_status_summary_zh: 小型模組化反應爐（SMR）是通常低於 300 MWe 的緊湊型核反應爐，設計為在工廠製造並於場址組裝。相較傳統反應爐，SMR 可能縮短工期，部署方式也更具彈性，特別適合偏遠地區、電網彈性不足地區，以及供熱等特定工業用途。全球有超過 130 種 SMR 設計正在開發，部分已興建或運轉，但尚無任何設計完成大規模複製部署。鈉冷式快中子 SMR 使用液態鈉作為冷卻劑，並採快中子能譜；不使用緩速劑降低核分裂中子速度，因此可利用未濃縮鈾增殖新燃料。由於液態鈉的特性，此類反應爐的熱效率高於傳統反應爐，可在低壓條件下運轉，系統也較為緊湊。目前尚無鈉冷式快中子 SMR 完成示範，更未大規模複製部署；但部分非模組化用途、輸出低於 300 MWe 的首批鈉冷式快中子反應爐已成功併網。未來小型模組化設計可利用這些既有反應爐累積的運轉經驗。
tech_0442_sodium-fast-smr.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 小型模組化反應爐（SMR）是推動脫碳的重要工具，可加快核能部署，並促進工業、製氫及 CCUS 等關鍵部門電氣化。SMR 也可部署於部分孤立地區或資料中心附近，協助提高電網彈性。
tech_0442_sodium-fast-smr.technology_status_summary_en:
A Small Modular Reactor (SMR) is a compact nuclear reactor (typically under 300 MWe) designed for factory production and on-site assembling. SMRs could offer faster construction than traditional reactors, and more flexible deployment. They are particularly suited to grid application, in remote or not flexible enough areas, and to specific industrial applications (heat production). Although over 130 SMR designs are under development worldwide, with some of under construction or already operational, not a single one has yet been replicated or deployed on a large scale.

Sodium Fast SMRs use liquid sodium as a coolant, and a fast neutron spectrum (no moderator to slow down the neutrons emitted by the fission reaction, enabling the breeding of new fuel from unenriched uranium). They have a greater thermal efficiency than conventional reactors, and can be operated in low-pressure conditions, in compact settings, thanks to the specific proprieties of the sodium coolant.

At present, no Sodium Fast SMR design has been demonstrated and even less replicated and deployed on a large scale, but some Sodium Fast Reactors, not intended for modular applications, have been successfuly connected to the grid in first-of-a-kind projects of less than 300MWe. The future development of reactors with small modular capabilities can benefit from the experience yield by these operated reactors.
tech_0442_sodium-fast-smr.market_dynamics_summary_en: SMRs are an important lever for decarbonization, enabling faster deployment of nuclear power and electrification of key sectors (industry, hydrogen production, CCUS). They can also help improve grid flexibility, for example by being deployed in certain isolated regions or near data centers.

Although over 130 SMR designs are under development worldwide, with some of under construction or already operational, not a single one has yet been replicated or deployed on a large scale.

Sodium Fast SMRs use liquid sodium as a coolant, and a fast neutron spectrum (no moderator to slow down the neutrons emitted by the fission reaction, enabling the breeding of new fuel from unenriched uranium). They have a greater thermal efficiency than conventional reactors, and can be operated in low-pressure conditions, in compact settings, thanks to the specific proprieties of the sodium coolant.

At present, no Sodium Fast SMR design has been demonstrated and even less replicated and deployed on a large scale, but some Sodium Fast Reactors, not intended for modular applications, have been successfuly connected to the grid in first-of-a-kind projects of less than 300MWe. The future development of reactors with small modular capabilities can benefit from the experience yield by these operated reactors.

They can also help improve grid flexibility, for example by being deployed in certain isolated regions or near data centers.

initiatives.description: In 2025, TerraPower raised 650Million USD, to further develop its sodium fast SMR, bringing its total equity financing to USD 1.5 billion

In 2026, the NRC officially issued the construction permit for TerraPower's Natrium reactor. TerraPower started constructing the general site infrastructure in 2024. This reactor is a 345 MW sodium-cooled one with molten salt storage using a HALEU fuel in metallic form.

In early 2025, Hexana secured EUR 25 million (equity and France 2030 grant) to finalize the conceptual design of its sodium fast SMR and submit related safety documentation to French regulators. Hexana’s platform—featuring two 400 MWth reactors and thermal storage for different industries— is targeted to be commercially available around 2035.

In 2023, Otrera, a French start-up developing a 50MWth sodium fast SMR for industrial applications was selected in France’s "Innovative Nuclear Reactors" call for projects under France 2030, following a successful initial funding round.

ARC Clean Technology is developping a 100MWe sodium fast SMR exploiting the experience gained during the operation of the experimental breeder reactor-II from the US government Argonne National Labs. In 2025, the company completed Phase 2 of the Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission’s (CNSC) Vendor Design Review, marking a significant step toward licensing.

Several pilot reactors had already been connected to the grid well before the Gen IV International Forum (Phénix in France and BN-350 or BM-600 in Russia). Although these were not intended to prove small modular applications, the experience gained greatly benefited research on sodium-cooled fast SMRs.
Blue Capsule, a french start-up, is developping a sodium cooled SMR for industrial application, in particular to decarbonise French cement production

In June 2025, the DOE’s Fuel Line Pilot Program was launch to establish a U.S. domestic nuclear fuel supply chain for testing advanced reactors, enabling private companies to build, operate, and license fuel production lines under DOE authorization. Oklo, a US startups developping sodium fast SMRs benefited from this program

Oklo is a US-based start-up developing sodium-cooled micro-reactors (Aurora) which targets commercial deployment as early as 2027. The company is, as ARC, exploiting the experience gained during the operation of the experimental breeder reactor-II from the US government Argonne National Labs. The start-up is achieving numerous milestones and key partnerships. In 2025 it received U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) approval for the preliminary safety analysis of its fuel fabrication facility and signed a binding contract with Siemens Energy to expedite the procurement of the power conversion system for its commercial power plant. In 2026, Oklo announced an agreement with Meta to support the development of 1.2 GW of nuclear energy capacity in Southern Ohio.
The Korea Atomic Energy Research Institute is developping a 100MWe sodium fast SMR for various applications (grid and off-grid electricity, industrial heat, district heating).

tech_0442_sodium-fast-smr.id: Nuclear > Fission > Small modular reactors > Sodium fast SMR
tech_0442_sodium-fast-smr.id_zh: 核能 > 核分裂 > 小型模組化反應爐 > 鈉冷式快中子小型模組化反應爐（SMR）

tech_0443_lead-fast-smr.technology_name_zh: 鉛冷式快中子小型模組化反應爐（SMR）
tech_0443_lead-fast-smr.technology_status_summary_zh: 小型模組化反應爐（SMR）是通常低於 300 MWe 的緊湊型核反應爐，設計為在工廠製造並於場址組裝。相較傳統反應爐，SMR 可能縮短工期，部署方式也更具彈性，特別適合偏遠地區、電網彈性不足地區，以及供熱等特定工業用途。全球有超過 130 種 SMR 設計正在開發，部分已興建或運轉，但尚無任何設計完成大規模複製部署。鉛冷式快中子 SMR 使用液態鉛或鉛鉍合金作為冷卻劑，並採快中子能譜；不使用緩速劑降低核分裂中子速度，因此可利用未濃縮鈾增殖新燃料。鉛的沸點高，並能提供良好的輻射屏蔽。首批商業化鉛冷式快中子 SMR 預計在約 500 °C 運轉，未來操作溫度可達 800 °C，支援製氫等不同工業用途。目前尚無鉛冷式快中子 SMR 完成示範。俄羅斯曾於 1960 年代運用鉛鉍冷卻核動力潛艦，日本也曾進行先進陸上實驗，但這些實驗成果無法完全直接套用至小型模組化設計。
tech_0443_lead-fast-smr.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 小型模組化反應爐（SMR）是推動脫碳的重要工具，可加快核能部署，並促進工業、製氫及 CCUS 等關鍵部門電氣化。SMR 也可部署於部分孤立地區或資料中心附近，協助提高電網彈性。
tech_0443_lead-fast-smr.technology_status_summary_en:
A Small Modular Reactor (SMR) is a compact nuclear reactor (typically under 300 MWe) designed for factory production and on-site assembling. SMRs could offer faster construction than traditional reactors, and more flexible deployment. They are particularly suited to grid application, in remote or not flexible enough areas, and to specific industrial applications (heat production). Although over 130 SMR designs are under development worldwide, with some of under construction or already operational, not a single one has yet been replicated or deployed on a large scale.

Lead Fast SMRs use liquid lead or lead-bismuth alloy as a coolant, and a fast neutron spectrum (no moderator to slow down the neutrons emitted by the fission reaction, enabling the breeding of new fuel from unenriched uranium). Lead, thanks to it high boiling point, has many advantages, such as the ability to provide good radiation shielding. First commercialised lead fast SMRs should operates at arround 500°C, but the operational temperature could reach 800°C, enabling applications in different industries (eg Hydrogen production).

At present, no lead fast SMR design has been demonstrated. Russia exploited in the 1960s Pb-Bi-cooled nuclear submarines, and advanced exeriments were carried out on land in Japan, but the results of these experiments are not totally transferable to small modular designing.
tech_0443_lead-fast-smr.market_dynamics_summary_en: SMRs are an important lever for decarbonization, enabling faster deployment of nuclear power and electrification of key sectors (industry, hydrogen production, CCUS). They can also help improve grid flexibility, for example by being deployed in certain isolated regions or near data centers.

Lead Fast SMRs use liquid lead or lead-bismuth alloy as a coolant, and a fast neutron spectrum (no moderator to slow down the neutrons emitted by the fission reaction, enabling the breeding of new fuel from unenriched uranium). Lead, thanks to it high boiling point, has many advantages, such as the ability to provide good radiation shielding. First commercialised lead fast SMRs should operates at arround 500°C, but the operational temperature could reach 800°C, enabling applications in different industries (eg Hydrogen production).

At present, no lead fast SMR design has been demonstrated. Russia exploited in the 1960s Pb-Bi-cooled nuclear submarines, and advanced exeriments were carried out on land in Japan, but the results of these experiments are not totally transferable to small modular designing.

They can also help improve grid flexibility, for example by being deployed in certain isolated regions or near data centers.

initiatives.description: Newcleo, a French start-up is making significant progress in licensing its lead-cooled fast reactor designs  (submission of safety program to French Nuclear Regulator in 2026, various submissions for Euratom safeguards review in 2025). The start-up also expands its partnerships (Oklo, JAEA, Blykalla…)
Russia and Japan have past experience in the development and operation of lead-cooled fast reactors, but not with on-land modular operations.

Blykalla has launched a pilot site in Sweden for its SEALER-E lead-cooled SMR prototype. The project, located near Oskarshamn, is supported by partners like Uniper, ABB, NCC, and the Royal Institute of Technology (KTH), with a SEK 99 million grant from the Swedish Energy Agency. The prototype will validate critical components and safety systems, paving the way for a SEALER-D demonstration reactor (80 MW thermal) and a future SEALER-55 commercial reactor (140 MW thermal) by the early 2030s. The company recetly raised USD 50 million in equity and announced a partnership with Oklo and Newcleo.
Russia is curently developing 100MWe lead-bismuth cooled SMR

tech_0444_molten-salt-smr.technology_name_zh: 熔鹽小型模組化反應爐（SMR）
tech_0444_molten-salt-smr.technology_status_summary_zh: 小型模組化反應爐（SMR）是通常低於 300 MWe 的緊湊型核反應爐，設計為在工廠製造並於場址組裝。相較傳統反應爐，SMR 可能縮短工期，部署方式也更具彈性，特別適合偏遠地區、電網彈性不足地區，以及供熱等特定工業用途。全球有超過 130 種 SMR 設計正在開發，部分已興建或運轉，但尚無任何設計完成大規模複製部署。在熔鹽 SMR 中，熔融鹽可同時作為冷卻劑、緩速劑及燃料介質。部分設計採快中子能譜，部分採熱中子能譜，另有設計結合熱中子與快中子能譜。這類反應爐的操作溫度可高於傳統反應爐。目前尚無熔鹽 SMR 開始興建，所有設計仍處於設計階段。美國曾於 1960 年代成功實驗運轉兩座先導反應爐，但當時並未以可複製的小型模組化設計為開發重點。
tech_0444_molten-salt-smr.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 小型模組化反應爐（SMR）是推動脫碳的重要工具，可加快核能部署，並促進工業、製氫及 CCUS 等關鍵部門電氣化。SMR 也可部署於部分孤立地區或資料中心附近，協助提高電網彈性。
tech_0444_molten-salt-smr.technology_status_summary_en:
A Small Modular Reactor (SMR) is a compact nuclear reactor (typically under 300 MWe) designed for factory production and on-site assembling. SMRs could offer faster construction than traditional reactors, and more flexible deployment. They are particularly suited to grid application, in remote or not flexible enough areas, and to specific industrial applications (heat production). Although over 130 SMR designs are under development worldwide, with some of under construction or already operational, not a single one has yet been replicated or deployed on a large scale.

In molten salt SMRs, molten salts serve simultaneously as the coolant, the moderator, and the fuel medium. Some design use fast neutron spectrum, other use a thermal one and other could use mix thermal/fast one. These reactors could operate at higher temperature than in conventional reactors. No molten salt SMRs have begun construction, all are still in the design phase. In the 1960s, two pilot reactors have been successfully operated experimentally in the United States, but without focusing on a reproducible small and modular design.
tech_0444_molten-salt-smr.market_dynamics_summary_en: SMRs are an important lever for decarbonization, enabling faster deployment of nuclear power and electrification of key sectors (industry, hydrogen production, CCUS). They can also help improve grid flexibility, for example by being deployed in certain isolated regions or near data centers.

SMRs could offer faster construction than traditional reactors, and more flexible deployment. Although over 130 SMR designs are under development worldwide, with some of under construction or already operational, not a single one has yet been replicated or deployed on a large scale.

In molten salt SMRs, molten salts serve simultaneously as the coolant, the moderator, and the fuel medium. Some design use fast neutron spectrum, other use a thermal one and other could use mix thermal/fast one. These reactors could operate at higher temperature than in conventional reactors. No molten salt SMRs have begun construction, all are still in the design phase. In the 1960s, two pilot reactors have been successfully operated experimentally in the United States, but without focusing on a reproducible small and modular design.

They can also help improve grid flexibility, for example by being deployed in certain isolated regions or near data centers.

initiatives.description: Stellaria, a French start-up backed by CEA and Schneider Electric, raised EUR 23 million in 2025 to develop its molten salt fast neutron reactor, aiming for commercial operation by 2035.

Kairos Power, a US based start-up is developping a 2*75MWe SMR using molten fluoride salt as a coolant. In 2025, the start-up signed a partnership with Google and TVA to deliver up to 50 MW from Kairos’ Hermes 2 molten salt reactor by 2030, supporting clean, reliable power for Google’s operations in Tennessee and Alabama.

In 2024, Kairos power started the construction of a 35MWth unit to demonstrate its Hermes reactor design. It's the first non-light water reactor design permitted for construction in the United States in over 50 years.
Moltex Energy is developing two molten salt SMRs, with non-conventional designs: the SSR-W and FLEX.
In 2022, Seaborg (Denmark, now Saltfoss) were awarded an EU innovation grant to advance their compact molten salt reactor (MSR) technology. In 2025 they are still designing their SMR.

Terrestrial Energy has signed an Other Transaction Authority (OTA) agreement with the U.S. Department of Energy to advance Project TETRA, a pilot reactor using its Integral Molten Salt Reactor technology. This OTA allows Terrestrial Energy to move rapidly from design to operation—bypassing traditional federal contracting constraints. The commercial IMSR plant design produces 822 MWth (390 MWe) of clean firm energy and is mainly designed for modular industrial heat production.
FLiBe Energy develops two molten salt reactor technologies using different fuels (low enriched uranium first, and thorium-based afterwards)

Řež, a Czech research center is developing a 20MWth molten salt microreactor designed for "operation in remote and populated areas and focusing on production of electricity, heat and hydrogen as a means of energy storage."

tech_0444_molten-salt-smr.id: Nuclear > Fission > Small modular reactors > Molten salt SMR
tech_0444_molten-salt-smr.id_zh: 核能 > 核分裂 > 小型模組化反應爐 > 熔鹽小型模組化反應爐（SMR）

tech_0445_heat-pipe-cooled-smrs.technology_name_zh: 熱管冷卻式小型模組化反應爐（SMR）
tech_0445_heat-pipe-cooled-smrs.technology_status_summary_zh: 小型模組化反應爐（SMR）是通常低於 300 MWe 的緊湊型核反應爐，設計為在工廠製造並於場址組裝。相較傳統反應爐，SMR 可能縮短工期，部署方式也更具彈性，特別適合偏遠地區、電網彈性不足地區，以及供熱等特定工業用途。全球有超過 130 種 SMR 設計正在開發，部分已興建或運轉，但尚無任何設計完成大規模複製部署。熱管式 SMR 透過密封熱管，被動地將爐心熱能傳送至動力循環；熱管通常使用鈉或鉀等鹼金屬作為工作流體與冷卻介質。此類設計可能適用於微型反應爐及太空應用。
tech_0445_heat-pipe-cooled-smrs.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 小型模組化反應爐（SMR）是推動脫碳的重要工具，可加快核能部署，並促進工業、製氫及 CCUS 等關鍵部門電氣化。SMR 也可部署於部分孤立地區或資料中心附近，協助提高電網彈性。
tech_0445_heat-pipe-cooled-smrs.technology_status_summary_en: A Small Modular Reactor (SMR) is a compact nuclear reactor (typically under 300 MWe) designed for factory production and on-site assembling. SMRs could offer faster construction than traditional reactors, and more flexible deployment. They are particularly suited to grid application, in remote or not flexible enough areas, and to specific industrial applications (heat production). Although over 130 SMR designs are under development worldwide, with some of under construction or already operational, not a single one has yet been replicated or deployed on a large scale. A heat‑pipe SMR passively transfers core heat through sealed heat pipes to a power cycle; these pipes typically use alkali metal working fluids such as sodium or potassium as coolants. This type of design could be suitable for microreactors and space applications.
tech_0445_heat-pipe-cooled-smrs.market_dynamics_summary_en: SMRs are an important lever for decarbonization, enabling faster deployment of nuclear power and electrification of key sectors (industry, hydrogen production, CCUS). They can also help improve grid flexibility, for example by being deployed in certain isolated regions or near data centers.

Although over 130 SMR designs are under development worldwide, with some of under construction or already operational, not a single one has yet been replicated or deployed on a large scale. A heat‑pipe SMR passively transfers core heat through sealed heat pipes to a power cycle; these pipes typically use alkali metal working fluids such as sodium or potassium as coolants. This type of design could be suitable for microreactors and space applications.

initiatives.description: Heat Pipe Reactors evolved from 1960s space‑reactor heat‑pipe experiments, through renewed 2000s NASA designs like Kilopower, to post‑2015 engineering breakthroughs enabling prototypes and commercial concepts such as Westinghouse’s eVinci.
The NASA KRUSTY’s 2018 full‑scale test proved stable startup, steady, and transient operation, validating safe autonomous control and readiness for future Kilopower flight demonstrations.
In 2025, the Westinghouse eVinci microreactor became the first to receive U.S. DOE approval of a Preliminary Safety Design Report, validating design maturity.

tech_0446_alternative-fuel-cycles-for-fu.technology_status_summary_zh: 部分核融合概念不使用氘氚燃料；若能成功，可消除部分燃料供應、反應爐耐久性、放射性及發電循環方面的挑戰。鋰氘與質子硼核融合可藉由帶電粒子，例如 α 粒子或質子，直接轉換電力，減少放射性廢棄物並簡化發電系統。質子硼及質子鋰反應可望實現無中子核融合，對面向電漿組件的耐久性極為有利，但需要極端反應條件。氘氦-3 核融合產生的中子也較少，但必須建立氦-3 增殖供應。
tech_0446_alternative-fuel-cycles-for-fu.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 這些替代燃料可與氘氚燃料一併評估，尤其可能在反應爐組件耐久性方面帶來顯著優勢。
tech_0446_alternative-fuel-cycles-for-fu.technology_status_summary_en: Some fusion concepts do not propose using deuterium-tritium fuel and, if successful, could eliminate some of the challenges associated with fuel supply, reactor durability, radioactivity and power generation cycles. Lithium-Deuterium and proton-Boron fusion offer direct electricity conversion via charged particles (alphas/protons), reducing radioactive waste and simplifying power generation. Proton-boron and proton-lithium promise aneutronic reactions (which is highly beneficial for the durability of the plasma-facing components) but demand extreme conditions; deuterium-helium-3 fusion, which also produce less neutrons, requires He-3 breeding.
tech_0446_alternative-fuel-cycles-for-fu.market_dynamics_summary_en: These alternative fuels are options to be considered alongside D-T fuel and could offer significant advantages, particularly in terms of the durability of reactor components.
tech_0446_alternative-fuel-cycles-for-fu.description: Some fusion concepts do not propose using deuterium-tritium fuel and, if successful, could eliminate some of the challenges associated with fuel supply, reactor durability, radioactivity and power generation cycles. Lithium-Deuterium and proton-Boron fusion offer direct electricity conversion via charged particles (alphas/protons), reducing radioactive waste and simplifying power generation. Proton-boron and proton-lithium promise aneutronic reactions (which is highly beneficial for the durability of the plasma-facing components) but demand extreme conditions; deuterium-helium-3 fusion, which also produce less neutrons, requires He-3 breeding.
tech_0446_alternative-fuel-cycles-for-fu.NZErationale: These alternative fuels are options to be considered alongside D-T fuel and could offer significant advantages, particularly in terms of the durability of reactor components.

initiatives.description: In 2023, Japan’s Institute for Fusion Science and TAE Technologies, a US start-up, reported the first fusion reaction using this fuel via magnetic confinement, following several examples since 2000 using inertial confinement.
In 2022, Electric Fusion Systems, a US startup, filed a patent for an apparatus that could potentially be used for proton-lithium fusion.
Helion Energy, a US based start-up planing a first operational plant by 2028 will use a D-He-3 fuel. For now, they're still using D-D fuel, for experimental work.

tech_0447_fusion-power-generation-cycles.technology_status_summary_zh: 在核融合電廠中，發電循環透過一次迴路，將發生核融合反應之電漿的熱能傳遞至熔鹽、氦氣或超臨界二氧化碳等工作流體。工作流體再驅動二次迴路中的渦輪機發電，二次迴路通常使用水。其原理類似核分裂反應爐，但操作溫度高得多，因此核融合發電循環具有獨特設計，尚未完成大型示範。這些循環目前也尚未針對高效率運轉完成最佳化。對核融合電廠可行性示範而言，效率不足或許尚非主要問題；但在考慮建設 GW 級設施前，仍須投入更多開發工作。目前也正研發不需要獨立發電循環的反應爐。
tech_0447_fusion-power-generation-cycles.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 若要在未來數十年部署核融合能源，須將更多研發資源投入約束系統的整合，並開發具成本效益的燃料循環及發電循環。
tech_0447_fusion-power-generation-cycles.technology_status_summary_en:
In a fusion power plant, the power generation cycle would transfers heat from the plasma (where fusion occurs) to a working fluid (such as molten salts, helium, or supercritical CO2) via a primary circuit. This fluid drives turbines in a secondary circuit (often using water) to generate electricity, similar to fission reactors but at much higher temperatures. The fusion power generation cycles are therefore unique and not yet demonstrated on a large scale.

These cycles are not yet optimised for high efficiency operation. While this may not pose a problem for demonstration of feasibility of fusion power plants, further efforts will be required before gigawatts of installations can be considered. Efforts are also underway to develop reactors that do not need a separate power generation cycle.
tech_0447_fusion-power-generation-cycles.market_dynamics_summary_en: For fusion energy to be deployed in the coming decades, a greater proportion of R&D efforts must be directed towards the integration of the confinement system, which requires the development of cost-effective fuel and power generation cycles.

tech_0448_fusion-energy-general-entry.technology_status_summary_zh: 核融合是使氫同位素等輕原子核在極端條件下結合為較重原子核，並釋放大量能量的過程。在太陽等恆星中，巨大重力壓力及高溫使氫原子核克服靜電斥力，融合形成氦並釋放大量能量。在地球上重現核融合極為複雜，因地球缺乏太陽的尺度與重力，無法自然加熱與壓縮燃料。數十年來，國際研究持續設計與開發可重現恆星條件的反應爐，以產生核融合反應並擷取能量。這類反應爐必須依需求將足夠高溫且高密度的電漿，也就是由帶電粒子與自由電子構成的流體，約束足夠長的時間，同時控制不穩定性，並確保釋放的能量多於消耗的能量。目前核融合研究主要聚焦氘氚（D-T）反應，分為兩種主要路徑：磁約束核融合（MCF）與慣性約束核融合（ICF）。磁約束利用強大磁場，在大型真空腔體內約束及控制電漿；慣性約束則以高強度雷射或離子束，在極短時間內將微小燃料丸壓縮至極高密度與溫度。自 1950 年以來，磁約束獲得最多預算與研究投入，但慣性約束近年也日益受到關注。另有其他技術路徑，目前仍屬較邊緣的研究方向。
tech_0448_fusion-energy-general-entry.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 核融合能源是無碳、可調度且安全的電源，可能為全球能源部門帶來顛覆性影響。若成功商業化，至 2100 年可能占能源結構的 30% 至 50%。然而，核融合開發時程漫長，大規模商業部署預期不會早於 2050 年，因此近期對淨零路徑的影響仍有限。
tech_0448_fusion-energy-general-entry.technology_status_summary_en:
Nuclear fusion is the process by which light atomic nuclei such as hydrogen isotopes are forced together under extreme conditions to form heavier nuclei, releasing vast quantities of energy. In stars like the Sun, immense gravitational pressure and high temperatures enable hydrogen nuclei to overcome their electrostatic repulsion and fuse, producing helium and vast amounts of energy. Replicating fusion on Earth is extremely complicated: Earth lacks the Sun’s size and gravity to naturally heat and compress the fuel. It has been the subject of decades of international research into the design and development of reactors capable of replicating stellar conditions, enabling fusion reactions and capturing the resulting energy. 

These reactors should confine on demand a sufficiently hot and dense plasma – a soup of charged particles and free electrons – for a sufficiently long time, while also controlling instabilities and ensuring that more energy is released than consumed. Current fusion research is largely centred on the deuterium–tritium (D-T) reaction and focuses on two distinct approaches: the first uses magnetic confinement (MCF), the second uses inertial confinement (ICF). In the first powerful magnetic fields trap and control the plasma inside a large vacuum chamber, in the second intense lasers or ion beams compress a tiny fuel pellet to extreme density and temperature for a very short period of time. The former has attracted the largest budgets and efforts since 1950, but the latter has gained interest recently. Other approaches exist, but remain, for now, more marginal avenues.
tech_0448_fusion-energy-general-entry.market_dynamics_summary_en: Fusion energy, a carbon-free, dispatchable and safe power source, holds disruptive potential for the global energy sector. If successfully commercialized, it could account for 30 to 50% of the energy mix by 2100. However, its near-term impact on Net-Zero pathways remains constrained by the protracted timeline required for its development—with large commercial deployment not expected before 2050.
tech_0448_fusion-energy-general-entry.description:

In stars like the Sun, immense gravitational pressure and high temperatures enable hydrogen nuclei to overcome their electrostatic repulsion and fuse, producing helium and vast amounts of energy. Replicating fusion on Earth is extremely complicated: Earth lacks the Sun’s size and gravity to naturally heat and compress the fuel. It has been the subject of decades of international research into the design and development of reactors capable of replicating stellar conditions, enabling fusion reactions and capturing the resulting energy. 

These reactors should confine on demand a sufficiently hot and dense plasma – a soup of charged particles and free electrons – for a sufficiently long time, while also controlling instabilities and ensuring that more energy is released than consumed. Current fusion research is largely centred on the deuterium–tritium (D-T) reaction and focuses on two distinct approaches: the first uses magnetic confinement (MCF), the second uses inertial confinement (ICF). In the first powerful magnetic fields trap and control the plasma inside a large vacuum chamber, in the second intense lasers or ion beams compress a tiny fuel pellet to extreme density and temperature for a very short period of time. The former has attracted the largest budgets and efforts since 1950, but the latter has gained interest recently. Other approaches exist, but remain, for now, more marginal avenues.
tech_0448_fusion-energy-general-entry.NZErationale: Fusion energy, a carbon-free, dispatchable and safe power source, holds disruptive potential for the global energy sector. If successfully commercialized, it could account for 30 to 50% of the energy mix by 2100. However, its near-term impact on Net-Zero pathways remains constrained by the protracted timeline required for its development—with large commercial deployment not expected before 2050.

initiatives.description: ITER, an advanced large-scale fusion reactor project is one of the most advanced experimental device. It was conceived in 1985 from a U.S.–Soviet initiative an in 2006, 33 nations formally launched the project in southern France. ITER aims to prove fusion’s feasibility at a large-scale. It seeks to produce ten times more power than consumed, sustain on demand high-temperature plasma, and test technologies for future bigger reactors (DEMO projects). Numerous experimental tokamaks (not intended to produce energy but to test components, plasma physics, fuel cycles, etc.) have been built or are under construction, partly to support the research efforts being made at ITER.
The IAEA estimates that in 2025, almost 30 fusion plants projects are planned. The development of these plants is fuelled by more than 150 experimental devices (mostly magnetic confinement systems).

In 2024, the ITER organisation updated its 2016 baseline, pushing back the achievement of full magnetic energy (the tokamak’s superconducting magnet system operates at its designed performance) by three years (from 2033 to 2036). This implies that D-T operations will begin in 2039.

Once ITER completes its experimental mission, the next step is the design and construction of a demonstration power plant (DEMO) to produce continuous electricity and achieve tritium self-sufficiency by 2050. Some member countries of the organisation are  planing the construction of such plants on their territory

In June 2025, as part of its new Clean Energy Industries Sector Plan, the United Kingdom announced the equivalent of USD 3.2 billion for a 5-year period in pursuit of a prototype fusion powerplant by 2040.

In 2025, the US DOE published its Fusion Energy Roadmap, a national strategy to deliver commercial fusion power by the mid-2030s by aligning public infrastructure, private innovation and industrial ecosystem. t identifies six core challenge areas — structural materials, plasma-facing components, confinement systems, fuel cycle, blankets and plant engineering/integration.
China has included the development of fusion energy among the key future industry objectives of its 15th Five-Year Plan covering the period 2026-2030.
The EU, India, Japan, Korea and Russia are also developing or planning to develop precise roadmaps for the commercialisation of fusion energy.
In its Hightech Agend Deutschland, Germany’s Federal Ministry of Education and Research indentifies fusion as a critical technology.

Major advances were made in magnetic confinement fusion in 2025, within public experimental devices. In Germany, a steady-state high-confinement plasma was maintained above key density and temperature thresholds using magnetic forces for more than 40 seconds. Longer records for plasma duration below this threshold were broken twice in early 2025, passing 18 minutes in China and then 22 minutes in France.
Using a laser-based inertial confinement approach, researchers in the United States recorded net energy output several times, including in 2025.

More than 80 start-ups are active in the fusion sector, a handful of which have raised a combined total of USD 10 billion in equity since 2015. Some have already begun designing or building power plants intended to produce scalable outputs. For example, Helion Energy (United States) began construction of its commercial power plant Orion in 2025.

tech_0449_magneto-inertial-confinement-f.technology_name_zh: 磁慣性約束核融合：磁化襯套（MagLIF）
tech_0449_magneto-inertial-confinement-f.technology_status_summary_zh: 磁慣性約束核融合結合慣性約束與磁約束原理，嘗試克服兩者的限制。此類方法整體上遵循慣性約束原理，但反應時間略長，並利用磁場加強燃料丸的加熱與壓縮。其原理可追溯至早期 Z 箍縮及 θ 箍縮研究，利用強電流或磁脈衝壓縮電漿；這些裝置證明，磁場可在快速壓縮期間協助穩定及約束電漿。磁慣性約束主要發展路徑包括磁化內襯慣性核融合（MagLIF）、磁化標靶核融合，以及以反場組態（FRC）為基礎的慣性核融合。在 MagLIF 中，電流脈衝沿燃料內襯流動，產生環繞磁場並壓縮電漿。此脈衝可由多種設備產生，包括阻抗匹配馬克斯發生器。
tech_0449_magneto-inertial-confinement-f.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 磁慣性約束系統的成熟度仍低於磁約束或慣性約束系統。然而，多家新創公司正建立商業化路徑；部分業者計畫藉由緊湊型設計與成本降低，在 2030 年前後完成首次商業示範。
tech_0449_magneto-inertial-confinement-f.technology_status_summary_en:
Magneto-inertial confinement systems attempt to combine the principles of inertial and magnetic confinement in order to overcome the limitations of both. In general, these approaches follow the principle of inertial confinement, with slightly longer reaction tines, and with a magnetic field augmenting the level of heat and compression on the fuel pellet. The principle traces back to early research into Z- and theta-pinches, where strong currents or magnetic pulses compressed plasma. These devices demonstrated that magnetic fields could help stabilize and confine plasma during rapid compression.

Magneto-inertial confinement is pursued through two main approaches: magnetized liner inertial fusion (MagLIF), magnetized target fusion and Field-Reversed Configuration based inertial fusion. In MagLIF, an electrical current pulse flows along a fuel liner, generating a surrounding magnetic field that compresses the plasma.  Various devices can be used to generate this pulse, including Impedance-matched Marx generator.
tech_0449_magneto-inertial-confinement-f.market_dynamics_summary_en: Magneto-inertial confinement systems remain less advanced than magnetic or inertial confinement systems. However, pathways towards commercialisation are being established by several start-ups, some of them planing first commercial demonstration arroun 2030 thanks to compact designs and reduced costs.

Magneto-inertial confinement is pursued through two main approaches: magnetized liner inertial fusion (MagLIF), magnetized target fusion and Field-Reversed Configuration based inertial fusion. In MagLIF, an electrical current pulse flows along a fuel liner, generating a surrounding magnetic field that compresses the plasma.  Various devices can be used to generate this pulse, including Impedance-matched Marx generator.

initiatives.description: In 2024, researchers at a US national laboratory reported a doubling of experimental energy yield from a single shot in a MagLIF device.
Pacific Fusion, an US based startup developing magnetized target fusion declared that they plan to reach positive net energy output by 2030. In 2025, they received a USD900millions funding round
In 2023, MIFTI Fusion, a US start-up founded in 2008, reported the highest neutron yield of any private company in experimental conditions.

Impedance-matched Marx generator is a key technology for pulsed-magnetic power that is generally applied with magnetised liner approaches. In 2024, Fuse, a US start-up, reported successful testing a generator that achieved a 330 GW peak power.
Zap Energy is a US based start-up developing a shared-flow-stabilized approach which reached the highest plasma pressure for this approach in 2025

tech_0450_magneto-inertial-confinement-f.technology_name_zh: 磁慣性約束核融合：磁化靶
tech_0450_magneto-inertial-confinement-f.technology_status_summary_zh: 磁慣性約束系統結合慣性約束與磁約束原理，嘗試克服兩者的限制。此類方法整體上遵循慣性約束原理，並利用磁場加強燃料丸的加熱與壓縮。其原理可追溯至早期 Z 箍縮及 θ 箍縮研究，利用強電流或磁脈衝壓縮電漿；這些裝置證明，磁場可在快速壓縮期間協助穩定及約束電漿。磁慣性約束的主要發展路徑包括磁化內襯慣性核融合（MagLIF）、磁化標靶核融合，以及以反場組態（FRC）為基礎的慣性核融合。磁化標靶核融合使用實體壓縮系統或磁場，壓縮預先加熱且磁化的電漿，以改善約束效果及核融合反應條件。
tech_0450_magneto-inertial-confinement-f.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 磁慣性約束系統的成熟度仍低於磁約束或慣性約束系統。然而，多家新創公司正建立商業化路徑；部分業者計畫藉由緊湊型設計與成本降低，在 2030 年前後完成首次商業示範。
tech_0450_magneto-inertial-confinement-f.technology_status_summary_en:
Magneto-inertial confinement systems attempt to combine the principles of inertial and magnetic confinement in order to overcome the limitations of both. In general, these approaches follow the principle of inertial confinement, with the magnetic field augmenting the level of heat and compression on the fuel pellet. The principle traces back to early research into Z- and theta-pinches, where strong currents or magnetic pulses compressed plasma. These devices demonstrated that magnetic fields could help stabilize and confine plasma during rapid compression.

Magneto-inertial confinement is pursued through two main approaches: magnetized liner inertial fusion (MagLIF), magnetized target fusion and Field-Reversed Configuration based inertial fusion. Magnetised Target Fusion compresses a pre-heated, magnetized plasma using a physical compression system or magnetic fields, enhancing confinement and fusion conditions.
tech_0450_magneto-inertial-confinement-f.market_dynamics_summary_en: Magneto-inertial confinement systems remain less advanced than magnetic or inertial confinement systems. However, pathways towards commercialisation are being established by several start-ups, some of them planing first commercial demonstration arroun 2030 thanks to compact designs and reduced costs.
